08001.name: Adams
08001.character: Established postwar suburban community with grid-pattern neighborhoods, mix of garden apartments and single-family tracts. Strong Latino community character. Dense commercial corridors along arterials.
08001.designContext: Mid-density residential scale preferred. 2-4 story buildings fit well. Community rooms and outdoor courtyards are high-value. Bilingual signage and culturally responsive amenities matter here.
08001.housingHistory: Rapid growth 1950-1990 as Denver's northern suburbs developed. Now infill-focused with strong affordability pressure from proximity to Denver and DIA employment.

08003.name: Alamosa
08003.character: Small high-desert valley town anchored by Adams State University. Historic downtown core with modest single-family neighborhoods, a notable mobile home share, and a significant agricultural and Latino population base.
08003.designContext: Single-story and two-story modestly scaled buildings are appropriate. Incorporate campus-adjacent design cues near ASU. Outdoor community spaces work well in the dry, sunny San Luis Valley climate. Culturally responsive programming and bilingual signage add value.
08003.housingHistory: Growth tied to the university and regional agricultural economy. Population has remained relatively flat; housing stock has aged, creating significant affordable housing need and limited new construction pipeline.

08005.name: Arapahoe
08005.character: Mature southeast Denver suburb with a well-established multifamily corridor along I-225 and along major arterials. Diverse and densifying, with a mix of garden apartments, mid-rise market-rate, and scattered affordable developments.
08005.designContext: 3-5 story multifamily buildings integrate well along transit corridors. Underground parking common in infill sites. Proximity to light rail stations supports transit-oriented design. Amenities should reflect multicultural resident base.
08005.housingHistory: Built out primarily in the 1975-2000 era as Aurora and Englewood expanded. Now experiencing redevelopment pressure, particularly near light rail stations. Strong LIHTC production history along the Colfax corridor.

08007.name: Archuleta
08007.character: Rural small-town community anchored by Pagosa Springs. High share of vacation properties and second homes drives housing cost pressure disproportionate to local wages. Large mobile home presence serves the working population.
08007.designContext: Smaller scale 1-2 story buildings consistent with mountain-adjacent small town character. Durable materials suited to mountain climate. Community gathering spaces and laundry facilities essential given mobile home conversion context.
08007.housingHistory: Growth tied to outdoor recreation tourism along the San Juan National Forest. Second-home market dominates recent construction, squeezing out affordable supply for essential workers in hospitality, healthcare, and schools.

08009.name: Baca
08009.character: Sparsely populated southeastern plains county with a ranching and farming economy. Springfield serves as the county seat. Extremely limited housing stock, high vacancy rates among aging units.
08009.designContext: Modest 1-story ranch-style construction blends with existing character. Durability and energy efficiency are paramount given extreme temperature range. Even a small workforce housing development would address significant unmet need.
08009.housingHistory: Population has declined steadily since the 1960s as agricultural mechanization reduced labor needs. Housing stock is aging, with limited investment in new construction or rehabilitation for decades.

08011.name: Bent
08011.character: Small Arkansas River valley county anchored by the city of Las Animas. Historically a farming and ranching economy with modest housing stock. Limited private investment in recent decades.
08011.designContext: Ranch-style and modestly scaled 1-2 story construction appropriate. Energy-efficient design matters given extreme plains climate swings. Essential workforce housing for healthcare and public services is the primary need.
08011.housingHistory: Population decline since mid-20th century mirrors broader southeastern plains trends. The county has chronic affordable housing gaps for the low-income population that remains.

08013.name: Boulder
08013.character: Affluent university city with very strong design standards and historic preservation. CU Boulder campus anchors the urban core. Tight supply constraints from strict growth limits produce some of Colorado's highest housing costs.
08013.designContext: High design standards required—projects face significant community review. Green building and energy performance certification (LEED, Passive House) are strongly favored. Missing middle and mixed-income formats near transit and campus are most viable. Affordable projects must address neighborhood compatibility carefully.
08013.housingHistory: Growth has been deliberately constrained since the 1970s via the Danish Plan and open space acquisition. Limited annexation and height restrictions have driven prices to among the highest in Colorado, creating severe workforce and family housing deficits.

08014.name: Broomfield
08014.character: Colorado's newest county, carved from the Denver-Boulder corridor. Primarily master-planned suburban communities with newer housing stock. Strong employment base at corporate campuses. Rapidly growing with increasing affordability pressure.
08014.designContext: Contemporary multifamily design with structured parking fits the newer suburban character. 3-5 story buildings along transit corridors. High design finish expected given affluent surroundings. Amenity-rich programming appeals to the working professional resident base.
08014.housingHistory: Broomfield became Colorado's newest county in 2001. Growth has been rapid and market-rate-focused. Affordable housing need is now emerging as land costs rise, and production has lagged behind job growth in the US-36 tech corridor.

08015.name: Chaffee
08015.character: Scenic mountain valley county anchored by Salida and Buena Vista. Rapidly becoming a lifestyle destination for Front Range refugees, driving housing cost escalation that local wages in outdoor recreation, retail, and healthcare cannot keep pace with.
08015.designContext: 1-3 story buildings consistent with small mountain town character. Natural material palettes, metal roofing, and covered outdoor spaces fit well. Salida's arts district character rewards design quality. Essential worker housing near employment corridors is the primary need.
08015.housingHistory: Historically a quiet ranching and mining legacy county. 2015-2025 saw dramatic in-migration from remote workers and retirees, pushing median home prices well beyond local affordability thresholds and intensifying the workforce housing crisis.

08017.name: Cheyenne
08017.character: One of Colorado's least populated counties. Eastern plains dryland farming economy centered on Cheyenne Wells. Extremely limited housing stock with no multifamily market to speak of.
08017.designContext: Single-story construction with durable prairie-appropriate materials. Even a small development of essential workforce or senior units would represent a meaningful intervention in this market. Energy efficiency matters greatly given the exposed plains climate.
08017.housingHistory: Population has declined for decades. The county's permanent residents are primarily ranchers, farmers, and public service workers. No meaningful housing development has occurred in recent history.

08019.name: Clear Creek
08019.character: I-70 mountain corridor county anchored by Idaho Springs. Serves as a gateway to ski country and a bedroom community for I-70 commuters to Denver. Tourism economy and second-home pressure limit affordable supply.
08019.designContext: Mountain vernacular architecture—pitched roofs, hardy materials, modest scale—is expected. Workforce housing near I-70 access points addresses commuter and service worker needs. Community connections and transit links to Denver important.
08019.housingHistory: Historic mining heritage defines the built environment. Population is modest but stable. Proximity to Denver and the ski corridor drives second-home speculation and limits affordable workforce housing development.

08021.name: Conejos
08021.character: Small San Luis Valley county with deep Hispanic heritage, subsistence farming, and an aging housing stock. Antonito serves as the county seat. Extremely limited economic base and housing activity.
08021.designContext: Adobe and vernacular Spanish Colonial design cues are historically authentic and well-received. Modest 1-story construction. Culturally responsive design and community gathering spaces are highly valued. Bilingual signage essential.
08021.housingHistory: One of Colorado's oldest settled counties, with land grants dating to Mexican territorial period. Population has declined steadily over the last 50 years. Persistent housing poverty with significant substandard housing units.

08023.name: Costilla
08023.character: Home to San Luis, the oldest continuously occupied town in Colorado. Deep acequia and land grant heritage. Very low incomes, high poverty rates, and significant substandard housing. One of Colorado's most underserved counties.
08023.designContext: Culturally sensitive design drawing on acequia and Hispanic vernacular traditions is essential. Adobe construction or stucco finishes are appropriate. Accessibility improvements and plumbing/electrical upgrades are often as important as new units. Community engagement must be deep and bilingual.
08023.housingHistory: Settled under Spanish and Mexican land grants in the 1800s. The acequia irrigation system and communal land traditions shaped the built landscape. Persistent poverty and population outmigration have left significant housing infrastructure needs unaddressed.

08025.name: Crowley
08025.character: Small Arkansas River valley agricultural county anchored by Ordway. Economy devastated by water rights sales from the 1970s onward. Among Colorado's poorest counties with very limited housing investment.
08025.designContext: Modest 1-story ranch-style housing appropriate. Senior housing and essential workforce units are primary needs. Energy-efficient construction matters for cost-burdened residents on fixed incomes.
08025.housingHistory: Water sales from Crowley County farmland to Front Range municipalities in the late 20th century dramatically reduced the agricultural economy and contributed to depopulation. Remaining population faces severe affordability challenges relative to incomes.

08027.name: Custer
08027.character: Remote Wet Mountain Valley county anchored by Westcliffe. An increasingly popular retirement destination that brings modest housing pressure. Very limited service economy.
08027.designContext: Rural mountain character—pitched roofs, natural siding, modest scale—is appropriate. Senior-oriented accessible design is a priority as the county ages. Even a small development creates a significant local impact.
08027.housingHistory: Small and historically stable population with light agricultural and ranching heritage. Growing retiree in-migration since 2000 has created modest housing demand but limited development response.

08029.name: Delta
08029.character: Western slope agricultural county anchored by the city of Delta. Fruit orchards and farm economy. Modest small-town character with postwar single-family housing predominating. Some retiree in-migration from Front Range.
08029.designContext: 1-2 story construction consistent with small agricultural town character. Community spaces and accessible design for an aging population are important. Affordable housing for farm workers and low-income seniors is the primary need.
08029.housingHistory: Fruit farming and the North Fork coal field shaped 20th century growth. Population is modest and relatively stable. Proximity to Montrose and Grand Junction provides some economic connection but limits local housing demand.

08031.name: Denver
08031.character: Colorado's capital and largest city. Highly diverse urban neighborhoods ranging from historic Victorian bungalows in Capitol Hill to contemporary high-rises in Union Station. Significant LIHTC production history. Acute affordability crisis driven by rapid population growth.
08031.designContext: Context is highly variable by neighborhood. Dense urban infill from 4-12 stories in core areas; 2-4 story compatible infill in residential neighborhoods. Transit-oriented sites near RTD lines preferred. Community spaces, bike facilities, and ground-floor activation matter. Projects in historic districts require compatible design.
08031.housingHistory: Denver's housing history spans 1860s Victorian subdivisions through the postwar suburban era and the 2010s urban development boom. Rapid in-migration 2012-2025 drove costs sharply higher. The city has the state's most active LIHTC, HOME, and local affordable housing fund programs.

08033.name: Dolores
08033.character: Remote Four Corners area county anchored by Dove Creek, the pinto bean capital. Extremely sparse population. Limited economic activity beyond ranching and farming.
08033.designContext: Modest 1-story construction blending with rural Southwest character. Even a handful of units creates meaningful local impact. Durable low-maintenance design essential given remoteness from contractors and maintenance services.
08033.housingHistory: Population has been declining for decades. The area was historically tied to mesa-top farming and ranching. Very limited housing development in recent history.

08035.name: Douglas
08035.character: One of Colorado's wealthiest and fastest-growing counties. Master-planned communities—Highlands Ranch, Parker, Castle Rock—dominate the landscape. Very high median incomes; affordable housing need is concentrated among service sector and healthcare workers.
08035.designContext: High-quality design and finish expected given surrounding affluent context. Contemporary suburban aesthetics with landscaping and community amenities. Mixed-income projects benefit from blending with market-rate character. Structured parking often necessary on smaller infill lots.
08035.housingHistory: Rural ranching land until the 1980s when master-planned development exploded along the I-25 corridor. Growth has been almost exclusively market-rate and upper-income. LIHTC production has lagged population significantly, creating growing essential worker housing deficits.

08037.name: Eagle
08037.character: Home to Vail and Beaver Creek, among the most expensive ski resort markets in the US. Extreme housing cost pressure with median home values far exceeding local wage capacity. Large Latino workforce population in mobile homes and overcrowded rentals along the I-70 corridor.
08037.designContext: Mountain resort design standards are high, with Vail's Swiss Alpine vernacular strongly influencing architectural expectations in the upper valley. Workforce housing in Edwards, Gypsum, and Eagle is more flexible in scale. Bilingual programming and culturally responsive amenities are essential given the large Latino service workforce.
08037.housingHistory: Vail was purpose-built as a ski resort in 1962. Rapid resort development created enormous employment with virtually no affordable workforce housing. Decades of under-investment in workforce housing have left the resort corridor with a severe structural deficit, now being partially addressed through inclusionary zoning and housing authority programs.

08039.name: Elbert
08039.character: Exurban county on the eastern edge of metro Denver. Predominantly large-lot rural residential—ranchettes and hobby farms—with pockets of suburban subdivision near Elizabeth and Kiowa. Residents largely commute to the Front Range.
08039.designContext: Lower-density residential character dominates. Small-scale 1-2 story multifamily in town centers is the most compatible form. Community space and storage important for rural households. Accessibility and transit connectivity to the metro are challenges.
08039.housingHistory: Low-density rural residential development accelerated in the 1990s as Front Range workers sought larger parcels. Septic and well infrastructure limits density. Affordable housing development has been minimal, with need concentrated among agricultural workers and low-income commuter households.

08041.name: El Paso
08041.character: Colorado's second-most populous county, anchored by Colorado Springs. Strong military community (Fort Carson, NORAD, Peterson SFB, Schriever SFB) shapes the rental market. Significant veteran population with specific housing needs. Conservative political environment influences housing policy.
08041.designContext: Suburban scale predominates—2-4 story garden-style and walk-up apartments are the norm. Military-adjacent properties benefit from secure access design and proximity to base amenities. Veteran-specific programming (peer support spaces, service coordinator offices) adds value. Southwestern architectural influences common.
08041.housingHistory: Growth accelerated post-WWII with military installation expansion. Colorado Springs has grown steadily since. Affordability has historically been better than Denver but pressure increased substantially 2015-2025 as migration from higher-cost metros arrived. LIHTC production is active, serving a large low-income renter population.

08043.name: Fremont
08043.character: Arkansas River canyon county anchored by Cañon City. Economy significantly shaped by correctional facilities (Colorado State Penitentiary, Fremont Correctional Facility, and others). Modest wages, older housing stock, and a retiree-friendly cost of living.
08043.designContext: Modest 1-2 story residential scale appropriate. Senior housing and workforce housing for correctional and healthcare employees are primary needs. Pedestrian-friendly design near Cañon City's historic downtown is well-received.
08043.housingHistory: Historic railroad and mining economy gave way to corrections as the dominant employer in the late 20th century. Population is stable but incomes are modest. Aging housing stock and limited development pipeline create affordable housing pressure for low-income residents.

08045.name: Garfield
08045.character: Western slope county anchored by Glenwood Springs and Rifle. Oil and gas boom-bust cycles have shaped growth. Mountain resort adjacency (Aspen is 42 miles away) drives housing cost pressure in Glenwood, while Rifle and Silt serve working-class oil field communities.
08045.designContext: Design character varies by community. Glenwood Springs rewards quality mountain-vernacular design given its resort-adjacent tourist economy. Rifle and New Castle can accommodate more utilitarian multifamily forms. Mobile home replacement programs are a significant opportunity throughout the county.
08045.housingHistory: Oil shale booms (1970s, 2000s) and busts shaped growth patterns dramatically. Resort industry employment in the upper valley creates permanent workforce housing pressure. The county has produced significant LIHTC developments in both Glenwood Springs and Rifle.

08047.name: Gilpin
08047.character: Historic gold rush mountain county anchored by Central City and Black Hawk. Limited-stakes gambling legalization in 1991 transformed the economy but drove residential displacement. Most workers commute from nearby counties.
08047.designContext: Historic district context is strict in Central City and Black Hawk—design review is rigorous. Workforce housing outside the historic core needs to accommodate casino and hospitality workers. Mountain-appropriate construction materials and durability standards apply throughout.
08047.housingHistory: One of Colorado's first settled counties during the 1859 gold rush. Gambling legalization spurred commercial development but eliminated much residential housing downtown. Workforce housing is significantly underprovided relative to employment.

08049.name: Grand
08049.character: I-70 mountain resort corridor county anchored by Winter Park and Granby. Significant second-home market. Strong ski and outdoor recreation economy with associated workforce housing crisis. Large mobile home presence serving seasonal workers.
08049.designContext: Mountain vernacular design expected across the county. Winter Park resort context demands high design quality. Granby and Hot Sulphur Springs allow more modest approaches. Durable, low-maintenance construction essential given harsh mountain climate and seasonal staff turnover.
08049.housingHistory: Resort development accelerated with Winter Park ski area expansion in the 1970s-90s. Remote work migration 2020-2025 brought year-round residents and pushed prices sharply higher, displacing service workers and deepening the workforce housing crisis.

08051.name: Gunnison
08051.character: Mountain county with a split personality: Western State University anchors Gunnison while Crested Butte caters to an elite ski resort clientele 30 miles north. Extreme housing cost pressure in Crested Butte contrasts with more modest but still tight conditions in Gunnison.
08051.designContext: Gunnison tolerates a range of building forms appropriate to a college town. Crested Butte demands mountain-vernacular design compatible with its Victorian mining heritage and ski resort aesthetic. Student and essential worker housing are the primary affordable need in Gunnison; seasonal workforce housing in Crested Butte.
08051.housingHistory: Gunnison's economy was historically ranching and coal. Western State's presence has stabilized the local economy. Crested Butte's transformation from a working-class coal town to a ski resort in the 1960s-80s created persistent workforce displacement. Remote work since 2020 has intensified pressure throughout the valley.

08053.name: Hinsdale
08053.character: Colorado's least populous county, anchored by Lake City. Extremely remote alpine environment. Economy based on summer tourism, hunting, and ranching. Virtually no multifamily housing stock.
08053.designContext: Any housing development is highly impactful in this extremely small market. Single-family and duplex forms are most compatible. Mountain-durable construction with low maintenance requirements essential. Even 8-12 units represents a meaningful addition to the county's housing inventory.
08053.housingHistory: Silver mining heritage from the 1870s. Population has never exceeded a few hundred permanent residents. Tourism and part-time residents account for most economic activity. Essential workers (county staff, healthcare, school) face severe housing scarcity.

08055.name: Huerfano
08055.character: Southern Colorado mountain foothills county anchored by Walsenburg. Historic coal mining legacy. Low incomes, aging population, and a significant housing quality deficit. Growing retiree and remote worker in-migration.
08055.designContext: Modest 1-2 story construction consistent with small mountain foothill town character. Rehabilitation of existing housing stock is often as needed as new construction. Senior accessible design is a priority. Energy efficiency improvements address both housing quality and utility cost burden.
08055.housingHistory: Coal mining drove 19th and early 20th century growth. Mine closures led to long population decline. Economy has partially stabilized around ranching, retirees, and modest tourism. Housing stock remains among the oldest and most deficient in the state.

08057.name: Jackson
08057.character: Remote North Park basin county anchored by Walden. Colorado's least densely populated county after Hinsdale and Mineral. Cattle ranching economy. Extreme winters. Very limited housing stock.
08057.designContext: Extremely durable, energy-efficient construction essential given North Park's harsh climate. Even a small development creates meaningful supply impact. Ranch-style single-story or modest two-story forms appropriate. Low maintenance design is essential given remoteness from contractors.
08057.housingHistory: North Park has been ranching country since the 1870s. Population has remained stable at a very low level for decades. Agricultural workers and county service employees face limited housing options.

08059.name: Jefferson
08059.character: Mature west Denver suburb with a mix of postwar single-family neighborhoods and more recent garden apartment corridors along major arterials. Strong outdoor recreation orientation given access to the foothills. Significant aging-in-place population.
08059.designContext: 2-4 story garden-style apartments fit the established suburban character. Mountain views matter to residents—design should orient outdoor spaces accordingly. Aging population benefits from accessible design and proximity to transit. Green building features align with the environmentally conscious resident base.
08059.housingHistory: Grew rapidly from the 1950s through the 1980s as Denver's western suburbs filled in. Now largely built out, with limited greenfield sites. Redevelopment of aging commercial corridors and underutilized surface lots represents the primary affordable housing opportunity.

08061.name: Kiowa
08061.character: Sparsely populated southeastern plains dryland farming county anchored by Eads. One of the smallest populations in Colorado. Limited service sector. Significant housing vacancy among aging stock.
08061.designContext: Single-story ranch-style construction appropriate. Any new development is meaningful in this extremely thin market. Energy-efficient design for extreme plains climate is critical. Senior and essential worker housing are the primary needs.
08061.housingHistory: The county has seen steady population loss since the Dust Bowl era. Agricultural mechanization and farm consolidation have driven outmigration for decades. Remaining residents face aging and deteriorating housing stock with limited repair resources.

08063.name: Kit Carson
08063.character: Eastern plains agricultural county anchored by Burlington near the Kansas border. Wheat farming and cattle ranching economy. Modest small-town housing stock with limited multifamily supply.
08063.designContext: 1-2 story modest construction appropriate to rural plains character. Senior housing is a primary need as the county ages. Energy efficiency important for cost-burdened households in extreme climate.
08063.housingHistory: Burlington grew as a railroad town and agricultural service center. Population has declined steadily. Agricultural workers and seasonal labor have limited affordable housing options.

08065.name: Lake
08065.character: Home to Leadville, the highest incorporated city in the United States at 10,152 feet. Historic mining heritage with a working-class community and a significant Latino population. Housing costs are rising due to mountain resort adjacency to Vail and Breckenridge.
08065.designContext: Victorian mining town character defines Leadville's historic streetscape—design should be compatible with the historic district. High-altitude construction requirements apply (wind loads, snow loads, energy performance). Bilingual programming and culturally responsive amenities are essential for the Latino community.
08065.housingHistory: Leadville was once one of the wealthiest cities in the US during the silver mining boom. Bust cycles led to depopulation and housing abandonment. Today the city is experiencing affordability pressure as resort workers priced out of Eagle and Summit counties relocate here.

08067.name: La Plata
08067.character: Southwest Colorado's largest county, anchored by Durango. Fort Lewis College brings university character to the community. Outdoor recreation economy (skiing, rafting, mountain biking) draws remote workers and retirees. Growing affordability pressure.
08067.designContext: Durango's small-city character supports 2-4 story mixed-use and multifamily development near the downtown core. Southwest architectural influences—stucco, earth tones, courtyards—are appropriate. Fort Lewis College proximity supports student and young professional housing formats.
08067.housingHistory: Historically a mining supply and railroad hub. Durango has diversified into tourism, higher education, and increasingly remote work. Housing costs have risen substantially 2015-2025, making workforce housing a significant community priority.

08069.name: Larimer
08069.character: Northern Front Range county anchored by Fort Collins and Loveland. Colorado State University drives a strong university-town character in Fort Collins. Excellent quality of life metrics and strong employment base drive rapid population growth and rising housing costs.
08069.designContext: Fort Collins has strong design standards and a pedestrian-oriented downtown. 3-5 story multifamily near CSU and Old Town is well-suited. Loveland accommodates more suburban-scale forms. Green building standards are expected—Fort Collins has aggressive energy code requirements.
08069.housingHistory: Fort Collins grew dramatically after CSU's expansion in the 1960s-80s. Growth has continued as northern Colorado's tech and agriculture sectors expanded. Affordability pressure has increased substantially, with LIHTC production accelerating to address the deficit.

08071.name: Las Animas
08071.character: Large, sparsely populated southeastern Colorado county anchored by Trinidad. Historic coal mining and railroad heritage. Significant Latino community. Low incomes and an aging housing stock characterize the market.
08071.designContext: Trinidad's historic downtown (a National Historic Landmark district) demands compatible design. Modest 1-2 story construction is appropriate throughout the county. Culturally responsive programming and bilingual services are important given the Latino community base.
08071.housingHistory: Coal mining drove growth in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Mine closures led to sustained population loss. Trinidad has recently attracted modest in-migration due to low costs and quality of life. Housing needs are concentrated among very low-income families.

08073.name: Lincoln
08073.character: Eastern plains county anchored by Hugo. Vast dryland farming and ranching landscape with an extremely sparse population. The county seat is the sole service center for a very large geographic area.
08073.designContext: Modest 1-story construction appropriate. Essential worker and senior housing are the primary development types feasible in this market. Durable, low-maintenance design is essential given remoteness from contractors.
08073.housingHistory: Homesteading-era settlement with modest growth through the 1950s, followed by population decline. Farming consolidation has reduced the agricultural workforce and economic base significantly.

08075.name: Logan
08075.character: Northeastern Colorado county anchored by Sterling. Agricultural service center with a modest multifamily stock. Northeastern Junior College adds some college-town character. Oil and gas activity provides employment variability.
08075.designContext: Modest 2-3 story multifamily development consistent with small agricultural city character. Senior housing and workforce housing for healthcare and agricultural sector workers are the primary needs.
08075.housingHistory: Sterling developed as a sugar beet processing center in the early 20th century. Growth has been slow but steady. Affordable housing pressure is moderate, with the primary need among very low-income families and seniors.

08077.name: Mesa
08077.character: Western slope's largest county, anchored by Grand Junction. Regional service center for much of western Colorado. Oil and gas boom-bust cycles strongly influence the housing market. Modest conservative small-city character.
08077.designContext: 2-3 story garden-style and walk-up multifamily is the dominant affordable housing form. Desert Southwest aesthetic with earth tones and xeriscape landscaping is well-suited to Grand Valley's semi-arid climate. Senior housing is a growing need as the regional population ages.
08077.housingHistory: Grand Junction grew as a railroad and agricultural hub. Oil shale booms in the 1970s and 2000s drove rapid but cyclical growth. The 2008 bust left significant housing inventory overhang. The market has since recovered with moderate but persistent affordability pressure.

08079.name: Mineral
08079.character: One of Colorado's smallest counties by population, anchored by Creede. Historic silver mining town turned summer tourist destination. Virtually no year-round multifamily housing. Essential workers face extreme scarcity.
08079.designContext: Historic mining town vernacular is the character reference. Any multifamily development would be highly impactful in this tiny market. Durability and low maintenance are paramount. Even a duplex or small fourplex changes the local supply significantly.
08079.housingHistory: Creede boomed in the 1890s silver rush and has since stabilized as a small year-round community with strong summer tourism. Essential workers—school teachers, healthcare staff, county employees—are the primary unmet housing need.

08081.name: Moffat
08081.character: Northwestern Colorado county anchored by Craig. Coal mining economy in long-term decline. High unemployment and income volatility following coal plant closures. Rural character with a modest small-city center.
08081.designContext: Modest 1-2 story construction appropriate to the small-city character. Economic transition context makes workforce retraining-adjacent uses and community service space programming valuable additions. Energy-efficient design addresses household utility cost burden.
08081.housingHistory: Craig developed as a coal and railroad town. The energy sector provided relatively high wages through the 20th century. Coal mine and power plant closures since 2015 have reduced incomes and created housing market softness while also leaving lower-income households in need of affordable stable housing.

08083.name: Montezuma
08083.character: Four Corners region county anchored by Cortez. Mesa Verde National Park draws tourism. Proximity to Navajo Nation creates cross-border economic connections. Significant Native American and Latino communities. Modest agricultural economy.
08083.designContext: Modest small-town character—1-2 story buildings are appropriate. Culturally responsive design and community gathering spaces matter for the Native American and Latino communities. Bilingual programming and culturally appropriate amenities are valuable.
08083.housingHistory: Cortez developed as a farming and ranching service center. Tourism growth tied to Mesa Verde has brought modest economic diversification. Housing affordability is a challenge for low-income families, particularly indigenous and Latino households.

08085.name: Montrose
08085.character: Western slope agricultural and small-city county anchored by the city of Montrose. Regional service hub for the western San Juan Mountains. Significant in-migration from Front Range and California is driving housing price escalation disproportionate to local wages.
08085.designContext: 2-3 story modest multifamily consistent with small Western city character. Southwest desert aesthetic—earth tones, stucco, xeriscape—appropriate. Senior housing and agricultural worker housing are primary needs.
08085.housingHistory: Montrose developed as an irrigation agriculture center in the late 19th century. Population has grown steadily. The post-2020 remote work migration significantly accelerated price growth, creating new workforce housing deficits in healthcare, retail, and hospitality.

08087.name: Morgan
08087.character: Northeastern Colorado agricultural county anchored by Fort Morgan. Sugar beet and dairy farming with associated food processing employment. Significant Latino immigrant workforce. JBS meatpacking plant is a major employer.
08087.designContext: Modest 1-2 story multifamily appropriate to the small agricultural city character. Culturally responsive design and bilingual programming are essential given the large Latino workforce. Affordable family-sized units with shared outdoor space are the primary need.
08087.housingHistory: Fort Morgan grew as a railroad and agricultural processing center. The growth of meatpacking and food processing has brought a significant Latino immigrant workforce whose housing needs have been underserved. LIHTC development has begun to address the gap.

08089.name: Otero
08089.character: Arkansas River valley county anchored by La Junta. Significant agriculture and food processing employment. Rocky Ford cantaloupe and Arkansas Valley agriculture define the economic identity. Modest small-city character with aging housing.
08089.designContext: Modest 1-2 story construction appropriate. Affordable family housing and senior housing are primary needs. Culturally responsive design matters for the Latino agricultural workforce community. Bilingual programming adds value.
08089.housingHistory: La Junta was a key Santa Fe Trail and railroad junction. Agricultural processing drove 20th century growth. Population has declined since the 1970s. Housing stock is aging and affordability challenges affect low-income agricultural worker households.

08091.name: Ouray
08091.character: Stunning historic mining town in the San Juan Mountains, known as the Switzerland of America. Ouray's ice climbing and hot springs draw year-round tourism. Extreme housing cost pressure relative to local wages. Very limited buildable land hemmed in by canyon walls.
08091.designContext: Historic district character is paramount—Victorian mining vernacular with strict design review. Very constrained building opportunities given topography. Essential workforce housing is the primary need. Any development opportunity is rare and should be maximized for density within character constraints.
08091.housingHistory: Ouray boomed in the 1880s silver mining era and has preserved its Victorian streetscape. Tourism-driven transformation since the 1980s has driven up property values. Essential workers—teachers, healthcare staff, tradespeople—are priced out of the local market.

08093.name: Park
08093.character: South Park basin county anchored by Fairplay. The highest county seat in the United States. Large recreational land base with significant second-home and vacation property ownership. Service workers commute from Buena Vista and Colorado Springs.
08093.designContext: Mountain vernacular character—pitched roofs, hardy materials, modest scale—is appropriate throughout. Essential worker housing in Fairplay is the primary affordable development opportunity. High-altitude construction requirements apply throughout.
08093.housingHistory: Historic gold mining heritage from the 1859 Pike's Peak rush. Second-home development accelerated from the 1980s onward. Remote work migration since 2020 brought new permanent residents and drove up prices, displacing service sector workers.

08095.name: Phillips
08095.character: Northeastern Colorado dryland farming county anchored by Holyoke. Grain farming economy with modest population. Very limited housing investment in recent decades.
08095.designContext: Single-story modest construction appropriate. Essential worker and senior housing are the primary needs. Even a small development is meaningful in this thin market.
08095.housingHistory: Homesteading-era growth with modest grain farming economy. Population has declined since the 1950s. Aging housing stock with limited new development activity.

08097.name: Pitkin
08097.character: Home to Aspen, Colorado's most expensive real estate market. World-renowned ski resort destination. Extreme housing cost crisis—median home values exceed $3 million in Aspen. Essential workers live in mobile homes, employer-provided housing, or commute from Garfield County.
08097.designContext: Aspen's design review is among the most rigorous in Colorado—Victorian and mining vernacular compatibility is required. Basalt offers more flexibility. Workforce housing programs here are sophisticated and high-investment. Mobile home community preservation and upgrade is a critical strategy. Down-valley sites in Basalt and El Jebel allow more practical formats.
08097.housingHistory: Aspen's mining heritage from the 1880s silver rush gave way to ski resort development beginning in 1946. The Aspen Institute's cultural cachet drew international wealth. By the 1990s, the county had become essentially unaffordable for its workforce. The Aspen/Pitkin County Housing Authority has operated one of the nation's largest deed-restricted workforce housing programs for decades.

08099.name: Prowers
08099.character: Southeastern Colorado county anchored by Lamar on the Arkansas River. Agricultural economy including sugar beets, grain, and cattle. Wind energy development has added modest economic activity. Small-city character.
08099.designContext: Modest 1-2 story construction appropriate. Affordable family and senior housing are the primary needs. Energy-efficient design addresses both climate extremes and cost burden for low-income households.
08099.housingHistory: Lamar developed as a railroad and agricultural hub. Population has been relatively stable. Housing affordability challenges are concentrated among very low-income agricultural worker and fixed-income senior households.

08101.name: Pueblo
08101.character: Southern Colorado's largest city, with a strong steel mill and working-class industrial heritage. Pueblo has significantly lower housing costs than the Denver metro, attracting in-migration from higher-cost areas. Significant Latino community. Older housing stock with rehabilitation opportunities.
08101.designContext: 2-3 story garden-style and walk-up multifamily is the primary affordable form. Pueblo's historic downtown and established neighborhoods have design character worth respecting. Outdoor spaces and community rooms are valued. Culturally responsive amenities and bilingual programming matter for the Latino community.
08101.housingHistory: Pueblo grew around the CF&I steel mill established in 1881. The steel industry's decline in the 1970s-80s led to population loss and depressed housing values. Recovery has been slow but sustained. Pueblo now attracts cannabis industry workers, retirees, and remote workers priced out of Denver, driving new but modest affordability pressure.

08103.name: Rio Blanco
08103.character: Northwestern Colorado county anchored by Meeker and Rangely. Oil and gas economy drives income volatility. Ranching and hunting culture. Very limited population density across a large geographic area.
08103.designContext: Modest 1-2 story construction appropriate for small western town character. Workforce housing for oil field and healthcare workers is the primary need. Durable, low-maintenance construction essential given remoteness.
08103.housingHistory: Rangely's oil field has driven boom-bust cycles since the 1940s. Meeker maintains a more stable ranching economy. Housing investment has followed energy sector cycles rather than community need.

08105.name: Rio Grande
08105.character: San Luis Valley agricultural county anchored by Monte Vista and Del Norte. Potato farming and ranching economy. Significant Latino agricultural workforce. Limited housing investment with high mobile home concentration.
08105.designContext: Modest 1-2 story construction consistent with small agricultural town character. Culturally responsive design, bilingual programming, and community gathering spaces matter for the Latino community. Farmworker family-sized units are a significant unmet need.
08105.housingHistory: The San Luis Valley's Rio Grande county developed as a potato and alfalfa farming hub. Agricultural workforce housing has been chronically underinvested. Mobile homes represent the de facto affordable housing stock for many low-income families.

08107.name: Routt
08107.character: Home to Steamboat Springs, a historic ranch town turned four-season mountain resort. The county retains a strong ranching identity alongside the ski and outdoor recreation economy. Workforce housing crisis has intensified as vacation home demand drove prices beyond local wage reach.
08107.designContext: Steamboat's western ranch-resort character—natural materials, pitched roofs, warm tones—is the design reference. Hayden and Oak Creek allow more modest forms. Mobile home community preservation is critical to maintaining the remaining affordable stock. Worker housing should prioritize proximity to resort employment corridors.
08107.housingHistory: Steamboat Springs transitioned from ranch supply center to ski resort from the 1960s onward. The ranching community's cultural identity has moderated but not prevented second-home displacement. Post-2020 remote work migration dramatically accelerated price appreciation and deepened the workforce housing deficit.

08109.name: Saguache
08109.character: Remote San Luis Valley county anchored by the town of Saguache. Very low incomes, high poverty rates, significant substandard housing. Great Sand Dunes National Park proximity brings modest tourism but limited economic benefit to permanent residents.
08109.designContext: Modest 1-story construction. Rehabilitation of existing housing stock is often as important as new units. Culturally responsive design for the Latino and long-tenured indigenous community populations. Even a small development is highly impactful.
08109.housingHistory: One of Colorado's poorest counties. Agricultural employment has declined while housing quality has remained poor. The county's remoteness limits access to construction financing and contractor capacity.

08111.name: San Juan
08111.character: Colorado's smallest county by population, anchored by Silverton. National Historic Landmark mining town. Growing year-round tourism economy (Durango & Silverton Narrow Gauge Railroad, Imogene Pass). Essential workers face acute housing scarcity in an extremely constrained physical environment.
08111.designContext: Historic district design review is strict—Victorian mining vernacular is the reference character. The physical topography severely limits new construction sites. Any affordable development is extremely impactful and likely requires creative approaches such as infill on existing lots.
08111.housingHistory: Silverton boomed in the 1880s silver era and declined precipitously after mines closed. The town has slowly revived through heritage tourism. Permanent population has hovered around 600-700 for decades. Healthcare workers, teachers, and tradespeople have virtually no housing options within the county.

08113.name: San Miguel
08113.character: Home to Telluride, one of Colorado's most exclusive ski resort communities. Victorian National Historic Landmark townsite with extremely high property values and virtually no affordable market-rate housing. Down-valley communities (Norwood, Placerville) serve the workforce at much lower cost.
08113.designContext: Telluride's design standards are extremely strict—Victorian compatibility is required within the historic district. Mountain Box Canyon physical constraints severely limit site opportunities. Down-valley in Norwood, more modest forms and materials are appropriate. Mobile home replacement programs in Mountain Village are a critical workforce housing strategy.
08113.housingHistory: Telluride was a silver and gold mining town that transitioned to ski resort starting in 1971. Unlike Aspen, Telluride's ski resort is locally owned and has invested significantly in workforce housing. Nevertheless, the housing crisis remains severe. The Telluride Housing Authority and San Miguel County housing programs have produced substantial deed-restricted supply.

08115.name: Sedgwick
08115.character: Small northeastern Colorado county anchored by Julesburg at the Nebraska border. Grain farming economy. One of Colorado's least populated counties. Very limited housing investment.
08115.designContext: Modest single-story ranch-style construction appropriate. Even a small essential worker or senior housing development is a significant market intervention. Low-maintenance, energy-efficient design is paramount.
08115.housingHistory: Julesburg was a Pony Express and railroad town. Population has declined steadily since the mid-20th century. The remaining population is largely elderly farmers and ranchers with modest housing needs.

08117.name: Summit
08117.character: Colorado's premier ski resort county with four major ski areas (Breckenridge, Keystone, Copper Mountain, Arapahoe Basin). Extreme housing cost pressure. Large seasonal workforce population in mobile homes and overcrowded rentals. Second-home saturation drives cost beyond local wage capacity.
08117.designContext: Mountain resort design standards are high throughout the county. Breckenridge's Victorian heritage requires compatible design in the historic core. Frisco and Silverthorne accommodate more flexible multifamily forms. High altitude construction requirements apply. Mobile home community preservation and replacement are critical to workforce housing strategy.
08117.housingHistory: Summit County's resort economy began with Arapahoe Basin in 1946 and accelerated with Breckenridge in 1961. Rapid vacation home development has crowded out affordable supply. The Summit Combined Housing Authority is one of the state's most active LIHTC developers, having produced thousands of deed-restricted workforce units.

08119.name: Teller
08119.character: Mountain foothills county anchored by Woodland Park and Cripple Creek. Woodland Park serves as a bedroom community for Colorado Springs commuters. Cripple Creek's limited-stakes gambling provides modest employment. Growing retiree and remote worker population.
08119.designContext: Mountain residential character—pitched roofs, natural materials—appropriate throughout. 1-2 story modest multifamily in Woodland Park and Cripple Creek fits the scale. Senior housing is a growing need. Proximity to Colorado Springs provides employment access for workforce housing residents.
08119.housingHistory: Cripple Creek was one of Colorado's great gold rush boomtowns in the 1890s. Woodland Park grew as a Colorado Springs suburb from the 1970s. Remote work migration since 2020 increased demand and pushed prices up, creating new moderate affordability pressure.

08121.name: Washington
08121.character: Eastern Colorado dryland farming county anchored by Akron. Grain farming and livestock economy. Very limited housing investment in recent decades. Small stable population.
08121.designContext: Modest 1-story construction appropriate. Senior and essential worker housing are the primary needs. Energy-efficient design is important for cost-burdened households in a harsh plains climate.
08121.housingHistory: Homesteading-era settlement with railroad-driven growth in the early 20th century. Population has declined steadily since peak in the 1950s. Aging housing stock with limited rehabilitation investment.

08123.name: Weld
08123.character: Colorado's geographically largest Front Range county, with a split character: urbanizing suburban communities in the south (Greeley, Evans, Windsor, Firestone) and vast agricultural and oil-producing areas to the north and east. Significant Latino community centered in Greeley and Evans.
08123.designContext: Greeley and Evans support 2-4 story multifamily consistent with mid-density suburban character. Culturally responsive design and bilingual programming are essential given the large Latino community. Agricultural worker housing in more rural areas may require simpler modular or manufactured approaches.
08123.housingHistory: Greeley was founded as a utopian agricultural colony in 1870. The JBS beef processing complex makes Greeley a major meatpacking center with a large immigrant workforce. Oil and gas production in the DJ Basin has driven income volatility. Affordability pressure has intensified significantly as Front Range sprawl reaches Weld County.

08125.name: Yuma
08125.character: Northeastern Colorado dryland farming county anchored by the city of Yuma. Grain and cattle economy with some wind energy development. Small stable agricultural community.
08125.designContext: Modest 1-2 story construction appropriate. Senior housing and affordable family units are the primary needs. Low-maintenance and energy-efficient design matters for the agricultural community.
08125.housingHistory: Yuma developed as a railroad and grain shipping point in the early 20th century. Population has been relatively stable. Housing needs are concentrated among elderly residents and low-income agricultural workers.
